questions.text: Identify the data structure that follows the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle.
Formulate the steps to insert an element into a stack.
Analyze the time complexity of searching an element in a linked list.
Identify the data structure that is suitable for implementing a queue.
Formulate the steps to delete an element from a binary search tree.
Analyze the trade-offs between using a hash table and a binary search tree for storing data.
Formulate the steps to implement a recursive algorithm for traversing a binary tree.
Identify the data structure that is suitable for implementing a priority queue.
Analyze the time complexity of sorting a list using the merge sort algorithm.
Formulate the steps to implement a dynamic programming algorithm for solving the 0/1 knapsack problem.
Analyze the trade-offs between using a graph and a tree data structure for representing relationships between objects.
Identify the data structure that is suitable for implementing a least recently used (LRU) cache.

answers.answer: 1. Check if the stack is full. 2. If not full, increment the top pointer. 3. Add the new element at the top of the stack.
The time complexity of searching an element in a linked list is O(n), where n is the number of elements in the list.

1. Find the node to be deleted. 2. If the node has no children, remove it. 3. If the node has one child, replace it with its child. 4. If the node has two children, find its in-order successor and replace it.

Hash tables have an average time complexity of O(1) for search, insert, and delete operations, but may have collisions. Binary search trees have a time complexity of O(log n) for search, insert, and delete operations, but require more memory and may become unbalanced.
1. Define the base case. 2. Define the recursive case. 3. Call the recursive function for the left subtree. 4. Call the recursive function for the right subtree.
The time complexity of sorting a list using the merge sort algorithm is O(n log n), where n is the number of elements in the list.
1. Define the base case. 2. Define the recursive case. 3. Create a 2D table to store the results of subproblems. 4. Fill the table in a bottom-up manner.

Graphs can represent relationships between objects with cycles, but may have a higher time complexity for traversal. Trees can represent relationships between objects without cycles, but may have a lower time complexity for traversal.
